notes.anchor: that famous clemency of his at Corfinium
notes.note: At Corfinium in February 49 BC Caesar captured the Pompeian garrison under L. Domitius Ahenobarbus and pointedly released the senators unharmed; this celebrated act of clementia was the cornerstone of his propaganda, which Cicero here flatters back to him.

The reciprocal salutation imperator to imperator: both men had been hailed imperator by their soldiers and so addressed one another as equals in military honour, a calculated courtesy on Caesar's part during his courtship of Cicero.

notes.anchor: those whom I let go are said to have made off in order to bring war upon me again
notes.note: The Pompeian officers freed at Corfinium, chiefly L. Domitius Ahenobarbus, who at once rejoined the war effort; Caesar professes himself unmoved, preferring (he says) that each side act according to its own nature.

notes.anchor: at hand to me near the City
notes.note: "Near the City" (ad urbem) means just outside the formal boundary (pomerium) of Rome; a magistrate holding military command, like Caesar or Cicero as imperator, forfeited that command on crossing it, so business was often transacted just beyond the line.
